content.assessment: From an institutional lens, status quo with monitoring leaves fully intact the selection architecture that produces the highest EU-level segregation and one of the most academically-selective school systems in the OECD (85% vs 43% average). Monitoring adds an information layer but does not touch any of the governance mechanisms—centralized admissions prioritizing grades/exams, church/private exemptions, early tracking ages—identified as structurally consequential.
opportunities: Monitoring infrastructure could later feed into targeted reforms of specific selection points without requiring immediate legislative capital
strengths: Requires no change to existing centralized admissions infrastructure, so administrative compatibility is trivially maximal
Monitoring could formalize measurement of segregation already visible in PISA-type data
threats: Continued coexistence of highly selective and non-selective tracks under one nominal system risks widening the sorting gap that already yields the EU's highest disadvantaged/advantaged segregation and a 12.1% disadvantaged-skill-attainment rate well below the EU average
weaknesses: Does not alter the selective-school share (85% vs 43% OECD) or early tracking ages (10-11, 12-13) that structurally sort students
Leaves unchanged the differential legal treatment of church/private maintainers that has previously enabled segregation outcomes (Nyíregyháza case)
